questions.question: The following resolution rule is used in logic programming. Derive clause (P v Q) from clauses (P v R), (Q v ~R). Which of the following statements related to this rule is FALSE? A. ((P v R) ^ (Q v ~R)) => (P v Q) is logically valid B. ((P v Q) ^ (Q v ~R)) => ((P v R) ^ (Q v ~R)) is logically valid C. (P v Q) is satisfiable if and only if (P v R) ^ (Q v ~R) is satisfiable D. (P v Q) => FALSE if and only if both P and Q are unsatisfiable
questions.answer: B. ((P v Q) ^ (Q v ~R)) => ((P v R) ^ (Q v ~R)) is logically valid
questions.to_find: Which statement about the resolution rule is FALSE

understand.plain: The resolution rule is sound: from (P v R) and (Q v ~R) you may always conclude (P v Q), because R and ~R cancel. A statement about resolution is true when it merely re-states this soundness or the way satisfiability is preserved. The FALSE option reverses the direction of inference, claiming the weaker resolvent implies the stronger original clauses, which it does not.

keywords.explain: An inference rule: from C1 v x and C2 v ~x derive C1 v C2; the complementary literal x cancels out.
keywords.example: From (P v R) and (Q v ~R), resolving on R gives (P v Q).

keywords.explain: An implication X => Y is logically valid (a tautology) when it is true under every assignment of truth values.
keywords.example: ((P v R) ^ (Q v ~R)) => (P v Q) is true in all 8 rows, so it is valid.

keywords.explain: A formula is satisfiable if at least one truth assignment makes it true.
keywords.example: (P v Q) is satisfiable: P = true already makes it true.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 460 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='30' y='20' width='160' height='44' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='110' y='48' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P v R</text><rect x='270' y='20' width='160' height='44' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='350' y='48' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Q v ~R</text><line x1='110' y1='64' x2='230' y2='130' class='edge' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='350' y1='64' x2='230' y2='130' class='edge' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='230' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>cancel R / ~R</text><rect x='150' y='130' width='160' height='44' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='230' y='158' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P v Q</text><text x='230' y='205' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>sound: premises => resolvent</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A resolution diagram showing two parent clauses (P v R) and (Q v ~R) combining by cancelling R to produce the resolvent (P v Q).

given.aim: Identify the one statement about the resolution rule that is not logically true.

terms.meaning: First parent clause containing the literal R.
terms.example: True whenever P is true or R is true.
terms.connects: Resolved with the second clause on R.

terms.meaning: Second parent clause containing the complementary literal ~R.
terms.example: True whenever Q is true or R is false.

terms.meaning: The resolvent derived by the rule.
terms.example: Follows from the two parents in every model.
terms.connects: Implied by the conjunction of the parents.

terms.meaning: Truth in every interpretation; the test each option must pass.
terms.example: Option A is checked by all 8 rows of P, Q, R.

given.plan: Test each implication for validity. Soundness gives A true and C true; the FALSE one reverses the implication so the resolvent would have to imply the parents, which fails.

steps.title: Confirm soundness (A is true)
steps.apply: If both parents hold: when R is true, (Q v ~R) forces Q; when R is false, (P v R) forces P. Either way (P v Q) holds, so A is logically valid.
steps.note: This is exactly the soundness of resolution.

steps.title: Test the reversed implication (B)
steps.formula_raw: ((P v Q) ^ (Q v ~R)) => ((P v R) ^ (Q v ~R))
steps.apply: Take P = F, Q = T, R = F. Antecedent: (P v Q) = T and (Q v ~R) = T, so antecedent = T. Consequent needs (P v R) = (F v F) = F, so consequent = F. T => F = F.
steps.note: A single falsifying row shows B is NOT valid.

steps.title: Check C and D, then conclude
steps.apply: C states satisfiability equivalence and D the standard 'empty-clause' characterisation; both are correct facts about resolution. Only B is false.
steps.note: B reverses the direction of the sound rule.

questions.formula_note: The resolution rule is sound (parents imply the resolvent), so the implication holds only one way; option B reverses it and is therefore the false statement.

questions.question: The following propositional statement is (P => (Q v R)) => ((P ^ Q) => R). A. satisfiable but not valid B. valid C. a contradiction D. None of the above
questions.to_find: Classify the formula (P => (Q v R)) => ((P ^ Q) => R)

understand.plain: We classify a formula as a tautology (always true), a contradiction (never true), or contingent. Here we test extreme rows. One assignment makes the whole implication true (so it is not a contradiction) and another makes it false (so it is not valid). Hence it is satisfiable but not valid.

keywords.explain: True under at least one assignment.
keywords.example: P ^ Q is satisfiable (P = Q = true).

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 480 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='20' y='20' width='200' height='150' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='120' y='46' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P=T,Q=T,R=F</text><text x='120' y='86' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>antecedent = T</text><text x='120' y='112' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>consequent = F</text><text x='120' y='148' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>whole = F (not valid)</text><rect x='260' y='20' width='200' height='150' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='360' y='46' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P=F</text><text x='360' y='86' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>antecedent = T</text><text x='360' y='112' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>consequent = T</text><text x='360' y='148' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>whole = T (satisfiable)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two assignment cards: one (P=T,Q=T,R=F) makes the formula false, another (P=F) makes it true, so it is satisfiable but not valid.

given.aim: Decide whether the implication is valid, a contradiction, or merely satisfiable.

terms.example: False only when P is true and both Q, R are false.
terms.connects: Left side of the main connective.

terms.example: False only when P, Q true and R false.
terms.connects: Right side of the main connective.

terms.example: (T, T, F) is one critical row.
terms.connects: Inputs whose values decide the formula.

given.plan: Find one row making the whole implication false (rules out valid) and one making it true (rules out contradiction); conclude satisfiable but not valid.

steps.title: Find a falsifying row
steps.formula_raw: X => Y is false only when X = T and Y = F
steps.apply: Take P = T, Q = T, R = F. Antecedent P => (Q v R) = T => (T v F) = T => T = T. Consequent (P ^ Q) => R = (T ^ T) => F = T => F = F. So the whole formula = T => F = F.
steps.note: Since some row gives F, the formula is NOT valid.

steps.title: Find a satisfying row
steps.formula_raw: X => Y is true whenever X = F or Y = T
steps.apply: Take P = F. Then (P ^ Q) = F, so consequent (P ^ Q) => R = F => R = T. A true consequent makes the whole implication T. So the formula is satisfiable.
steps.note: Some row gives T, so it is NOT a contradiction.

steps.title: Classify
steps.apply: True somewhere and false somewhere means it is satisfiable but not valid.

solution.result: The statement is satisfiable but not valid (option A).

questions.formula_note: Probing the two extreme implication rows (antecedent true / consequent false, and antecedent false) settles validity vs satisfiability instantly.

questions.question: Let P, Q, and R be three atomic propositional assertions. Let X denote (P v Q) -> R and Y denote (P -> R) v (Q -> R). Which one of the following is a tautology? A. X = Y B. X -> Y C. Y -> X D. ~Y -> X
questions.to_find: Which relation between X and Y is a tautology

understand.plain: X says 'if P or Q then R'; Y says 'P implies R, or Q implies R'. Whenever X is true, Y must also be true, so X -> Y is always true (a tautology). The reverse fails, and equivalence fails, because Y can be true while X is false. So only X -> Y is a tautology.

keywords.explain: A -> B is equivalent to ~A v B; it is false only when A is true and B is false.
A formula true in every row of its truth table.
keywords.example: X -> Y is true for all 8 assignments of P, Q, R.

keywords.explain: A single assignment that falsifies a claimed tautology, disproving it.
keywords.example: P=T, Q=F, R=F makes Y true but X false, killing Y -> X.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 480 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='30' y='40' width='150' height='60' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='105' y='66' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>X</text><text x='105' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>(P v Q) -> R</text><rect x='300' y='40' width='150' height='60' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='375' y='66' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Y</text><text x='375' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>(P->R) v (Q->R)</text><line x1='180' y1='70' x2='300' y2='70' class='edge' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><text x='240' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>X => Y always</text><text x='240' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>Y => X fails (P=T,Q=F,R=F)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: X (stronger) always implies Y (weaker); the reverse arrow Y -> X is broken by the counterexample P=T, Q=F, R=F.

given.aim: Determine which of the four relations between X and Y holds in every interpretation.

terms.meaning: (P v Q) -> R: if either P or Q holds, then R.
terms.example: X is false at P=T, Q=F, R=F.

terms.meaning: (P -> R) v (Q -> R): at least one of the two implications holds.
terms.example: Y is true at P=T, Q=F, R=F because Q -> R = T.
(T, F, F) is the decisive counterexample row.
terms.connects: Drive the truth values of X and Y.

given.plan: Show X true forces Y true (so X -> Y is a tautology), and exhibit a row where Y is true but X is false to rule out Y -> X, equivalence, and ~Y -> X.

steps.title: Show X -> Y is a tautology
steps.formula_raw: X = (P v Q) -> R ; Y = (P -> R) v (Q -> R)
steps.apply: Suppose X is true. If R is true, both P->R and Q->R are true, so Y is true. If R is false, X true forces (P v Q) false, i.e. P and Q both false, so P->R and Q->R are both true (vacuously), hence Y is true. Thus X true => Y true always.
steps.note: No row has X true and Y false, so X -> Y is valid.

steps.title: Refute the other options
steps.formula_raw: counterexample: P = T, Q = F, R = F
steps.apply: Here X = (T v F) -> F = T -> F = F, while Y = (T->F) v (F->F) = F v T = T. So Y = T, X = F: this breaks Y -> X (T -> F = F) and X = Y; and ~Y -> X = F -> F is true here but fails elsewhere (e.g. P=T,Q=T,R=F gives Y=F so ~Y=T, X=F, ~Y->X = F).
steps.note: Only X -> Y survives every row.

solution.result: X -> Y is the tautology (option B).

questions.formula_note: X is logically stronger than Y, so X -> Y is a tautology while the converse and equivalence are falsified by P=T, Q=F, R=F.

questions.question: Consider the following propositional statements: P_1 : ((A ^ B) -> C) = ((A -> C) ^ (B -> C)) P_2 : ((A v B) -> C) = ((A -> C) ^ (B -> C)). Which one of the following is true? A. P_1 is a tautology, but not P_2 B. P_2 is a tautology, but not P_1 C. P_1 and P_2 are both tautologies D. Both P_1 and P_2 are not tautologies
questions.answer: D. Both P_1 and P_2 are not tautologies
questions.to_find: Whether the biconditionals P_1 and P_2 are tautologies

understand.plain: Each statement claims a biconditional (an equivalence) between two formulas. The statement is a tautology only if the two sides agree in every row. P_1 fails because (A ^ B) -> C does not equal (A -> C) ^ (B -> C). Per the official GATE key the answer is D; note that by standard truth tables P_2 is actually valid, so this question is widely regarded as having a debatable key.

keywords.explain: X = Y (X iff Y) is true exactly when X and Y have the same truth value.
keywords.example: P = P is always true; P = ~P is never true.

keywords.explain: Implication distributes nicely over a disjunctive antecedent: (A v B) -> C equals (A->C) ^ (B->C), but not over a conjunctive one.
keywords.example: (A v B) -> C means both A and B separately force C.

keywords.explain: One assignment where the two sides differ disproves the claimed equivalence.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 500 210' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='20' y='20' width='220' height='170' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='130' y='46' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P_1</text><text x='130' y='76' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>(A^B)->C  vs</text><text x='130' y='98' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>(A->C)^(B->C)</text><text x='130' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>A=T,B=F,C=F:</text><text x='130' y='162' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>T vs F  => differ</text><rect x='260' y='20' width='220' height='170' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='370' y='46' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P_2</text><text x='370' y='76' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>(AvB)->C  vs</text><text x='370' y='98' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>(A->C)^(B->C)</text><text x='370' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>sides match every row</text><text x='370' y='162' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>(official key: not taut.)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two cards comparing P_1 and P_2; P_1 clearly differs at A=T,B=F,C=F, while P_2's two sides coincide though the official key still marks D.

given.aim: Decide which of P_1 and P_2 are tautologies according to the official key.

terms.meaning: If both A and B hold, then C.
terms.example: False only at A=T, B=T, C=F.
terms.connects: Left side of the P_1 biconditional.

terms.meaning: If at least one of A, B holds, then C.
terms.connects: Left side of the P_2 biconditional.

terms.example: Equals 'A and B each force C'.
terms.connects: Shared right side of both biconditionals.

given.plan: Falsify P_1 with one row; report the answer as D per the authoritative key while flagging the standard semantic result for P_2.

steps.title: Disprove P_1
steps.apply: Take A = T, B = F, C = F. Left: (A ^ B) -> C = (T ^ F) -> F = F -> F = T. Right: (A -> C) ^ (B -> C) = (T -> F) ^ (F -> F) = F ^ T = F. Left = T, Right = F, so the biconditional is false here.

steps.title: Examine P_2 and apply the official key
steps.apply: By standard semantics the two sides of P_2 agree in all 8 rows, so P_2 is in fact valid. However the authoritative answer key for this question records the option as D ('both not tautologies'), so the recorded correct option is D.
steps.note: Eliminating A and C (which assume P_1 holds) leaves only B or D; since the key is D, we report D.

solution.result: Per the official key: both P_1 and P_2 are not tautologies (option D).

questions.formula_note: P_1 fails at A=T,B=F,C=F; by standard truth tables P_2 is actually valid, but the official GATE key marks the answer D, which we follow here.

questions.question: P and Q are two propositions. Which of the following logical expressions are equivalent? I. P v ~Q II. ~(~P ^ Q) III. (P ^ Q) v (P ^ ~Q) v (~P ^ ~Q) IV. (P ^ Q) v (P ^ ~Q) v (~P ^ Q). A. Only I and II B. Only I, II and III C. Only I, II and IV D. All of I, II, III and IV
questions.answer: B. Only I, II and III
questions.to_find: Which of the four expressions are logically equivalent

understand.plain: We compare the four expressions over the 4 rows of P and Q. Expressions I and II are equal by De Morgan's law. Expression III is the sum of three minterms that turns out to equal P v ~Q as well, while IV uses a different minterm and equals P v Q instead. So I, II and III match, but IV does not.

keywords.example: ~(~P ^ Q) = P v ~Q, so II equals I.

keywords.explain: A product term that is true for exactly one row of the truth table (e.g. P ^ ~Q).
keywords.example: P ^ ~Q is true only at P=T, Q=F.

keywords.explain: OR-ing minterms reconstructs a function from the rows where it is true.
keywords.example: III = three minterms = all rows except P=F,Q=T = P v ~Q.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 500 230' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='250' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Truth vectors over (P,Q) = TT,TF,FT,FF</text><rect x='30' y='50' width='200' height='40' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='130' y='76' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>I, II, III: T  T  F  T</text><rect x='270' y='50' width='200' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='370' y='76' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>IV: T  T  T  F</text><text x='130' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>I = II = III  (= P v ~Q)</text><text x='370' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>IV = P v Q  (differs)</text><text x='250' y='170' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>F only at P=F,Q=T for I/II/III</text><text x='250' y='196' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>F only at P=F,Q=F for IV</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Truth vectors show I, II, III all equal T,T,F,T (= P v ~Q), while IV is T,T,T,F (= P v Q), so only I, II, III are equivalent.

given.aim: Find the set of expressions that share the same truth table.

terms.meaning: True unless P is false and Q is true.
Negation of (~P ^ Q); by De Morgan equals P v ~Q.
Sum of minterms for rows TT, TF, FF.
terms.example: True everywhere except FT, vector T,T,F,T.

terms.meaning: Sum of minterms for rows TT, TF, FT.
terms.example: True everywhere except FF, vector T,T,T,F.
terms.connects: Equals P v Q, different from the rest.

given.plan: Build the 4-row truth vector of each expression; group the ones whose vectors coincide.

steps.title: Reduce I and II by De Morgan
steps.apply: II = ~(~P ^ Q) simplifies directly to P v ~Q, which is exactly I. So I and II are equivalent with truth vector (over TT,TF,FT,FF) = T, T, F, T.
steps.note: I and II are the same.

steps.title: Evaluate III as a sum of minterms
steps.apply: These three minterms are true at rows TT, TF, FF respectively. So III is true on {TT, TF, FF} and false only at FT, giving vector T, T, F, T = P v ~Q. Hence III equals I and II.

steps.title: Evaluate IV and compare
steps.apply: Its minterms are true at TT, TF, FT, so IV is false only at FF, giving vector T, T, T, F = P v Q. This differs from T, T, F, T, so IV is not equivalent to the others.
steps.note: Only I, II, III are equivalent.

solution.result: Only I, II and III are equivalent (option B).

questions.formula_note: De Morgan gives I = II = P v ~Q, and the minterm sums show III = P v ~Q but IV = P v Q, so exactly I, II, III match.

formulas.name: Resolution Inference Rule
formulas.plain: From the clauses (P v R) and (Q v ~R), the complementary literals R and ~R cancel, allowing you to derive the resolvent (P v Q).
formulas.whenToUse: When deriving new clauses in propositional resolution / logic programming, or when checking the soundness of a clause derivation.

terms.means: Arbitrary literals or sub-clauses carried over into the resolvent.
The complementary pair of literals that cancel during resolution.

formulas.trap: Resolution is sound only in the forward direction: the parents imply the resolvent, NOT the other way round. Reversing the implication is invalid.

formulas.name: Truth Condition of Material Implication
formulas.plain: An implication X -> Y is false only when X is true and Y is false; in every other case it is true. Equivalently X -> Y equals ~X v Y.
formulas.whenToUse: When testing whether an implication is valid, satisfiable, or false, or when rewriting implications using disjunction.
formulas.trap: A false antecedent makes the implication vacuously true; do not read X -> Y as requiring X to hold.
formulas.reference: Propositional logic, truth tables for the conditional connective.

formulas.name: Logical Equivalence via Truth Tables
formulas.latex: X \equiv Y \iff (X \leftrightarrow Y) \text{ is a tautology}
formulas.plain: Two formulas are logically equivalent when they have identical truth values in every row of the truth table; a single differing row disproves equivalence.
formulas.whenToUse: When deciding whether a biconditional is a tautology or whether two expressions can be interchanged.

terms.means: The two propositional formulas being compared.
Biconditional connective; true exactly when both sides agree.

formulas.trap: One counterexample row is enough to break equivalence; you need not check all rows once a mismatch is found.
formulas.reference: Propositional logic, equivalence and tautology checking.

formulas.name: De Morgan's Laws
formulas.latex: \lnot(X \land Y) \equiv \lnot X \lor \lnot Y,\quad \lnot(X \lor Y) \equiv \lnot X \land \lnot Y
formulas.plain: Negating a conjunction flips it to a disjunction of negations, and vice versa. Pushing a negation inward swaps AND with OR and negates each part.
formulas.whenToUse: When simplifying negated compound expressions or proving equivalences between logical formulas.

terms.means: The two propositions inside the negated connective.

formulas.trap: Remember to negate BOTH operands and switch the connective; forgetting either step is the usual error.

formulas.name: Sum-of-Minterms Truth Vector
formulas.plain: A function written as an OR of minterms is true exactly on the rows whose minterms appear in the sum; listing those rows gives the function's truth vector directly.
formulas.whenToUse: When comparing expressions given in sum-of-products form, or reconstructing a function from the rows where it is true.

terms.means: A minterm, true on exactly one assignment of the variables.
The set of rows (assignments) where the function is true.

formulas.trap: Each minterm pins down one specific row; mixing up which assignment a minterm represents corrupts the whole truth vector.
formulas.reference: Boolean algebra / propositional logic, canonical sum-of-products.
